skill.name: web-auth
skill.description: React authentication guidance covering cookie-based sessions, OAuth, NextAuth.js, protected routes, token refresh, forms, CSRF, headers, and FastAPI integration.
skill.summary: Build React authentication flows with reusable patterns for sessions, OAuth, protected routes, and security controls.
skill.author: CJHarmath

security_audit.summary: The skill is Markdown reference content and does not execute shell commands; the reported backtick execution is from fenced code blocks. Network and environment-variable patterns are legitimate authentication examples, not confirmed exfiltration. One protected-route example only checks for a cookie, so copied production use needs stronger token or session validation.

medium_findings.title: Incomplete Token Validation in Middleware Example
medium_findings.description: The Next.js middleware example reads an auth-token cookie and only checks whether it exists before allowing dashboard paths. A copied implementation could allow requests with any cookie value unless signed token verification or backend session validation is added.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The control flow clearly gates access on token presence only. It is example code, so the risk depends on whether users copy it without adding validation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences
low_findings.description: The static external-command alerts point to Markdown fenced code block delimiters and TypeScript examples. No evidence found of executable Ruby backticks or shell execution in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations align with Markdown code fences and surrounding documentation. The file contains reference snippets, not runnable shell commands.

low_findings.title: Network Calls Are Authentication Examples
low_findings.description: The fetch calls target local application authentication endpoints or the documented Google Identity Services script. No evidence found that the skill sends credentials to an unauthorized external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network examples are consistent with the stated authentication purpose. They use relative app endpoints except for the expected Google OAuth script.

low_findings.title: Environment Variables Used for Provider Configuration
low_findings.description: The process.env references are sample configuration for Google OAuth client IDs and secrets. This is normal for server-side auth configuration, and no evidence found of secret logging or network exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variables are used as provider inputs in authentication setup examples. The public client ID is browser-safe, while the secret appears in a NextAuth server configuration example.

low_findings.title: Browser Storage Mentions Are Cautionary
low_findings.description: The localStorage references describe its XSS exposure and recommend moving tokens to httpOnly cookies. No evidence found that the skill instructs users to store long-lived secrets in browser storage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both flagged lines explicitly frame localStorage as less safe or as an issue to fix. The recommended pattern is httpOnly cookies.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alerts
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar algorithms. The cited lines contain metadata, redirect guidance, and an HSTS max-age value.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found no weak cryptographic primitive. The static match appears to be keyword-based.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Alerts
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts point to normal identity fields, validation messages, and authentication errors. No evidence found of host, user, network, or filesystem reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is ordinary authentication sample code. It does not collect system metadata or probe the execution environment.

content.value_statement: React authentication is hard to assemble safely across clients, routes, providers, and backends. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for common web auth flows.
actual_capabilities: Explains cookie-based authentication clients that send credentials with requests.
Provides a React AuthContext pattern for user state, login, logout, and refresh.
Shows Google OAuth and NextAuth.js setup patterns for React and Next.js apps.
Demonstrates protected route patterns for React Router and Next.js App Router.
Covers token refresh, login form validation, CSRF headers, and security headers.
Includes a FastAPI example for issuing and clearing httpOnly auth cookies.
limitations: It is reference guidance, not a complete authentication library.
Examples need adaptation to each app, framework version, and identity provider.
Backend helpers such as credential checks and token creation are placeholders.
Production deployments still need security review, tests, rate limits, and monitoring.

use_cases.title: Add Login to a React App
use_cases.description: Use the cookie client and AuthContext patterns to structure sign-in, sign-out, and user loading states.

use_cases.title: Connect OAuth in Next.js
use_cases.description: Use the Google OAuth and NextAuth.js examples to plan provider configuration and session access.

use_cases.title: Review Auth Flow Coverage
use_cases.description: Compare an existing app against the route protection, refresh, CSRF, and cookie guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Auth Context
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for client-side auth state.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the web-auth skill to design a React AuthContext for login, logout, current user loading, and authenticated state.

prompt_templates.title: Protect Application Routes
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate routing work in a React app.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the web-auth skill to add protected routes to my React Router app and preserve the destination after login.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate OAuth with NextAuth.js
prompt_templates.scenario: Provider-based authentication for a Next.js app.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the web-auth skill to plan a NextAuth.js setup with Google OAuth and credentials login for my Next.js app.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Production Auth Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the web-auth skill to review my React authentication design for cookie settings, CSRF, token refresh, protected routes, and backend validation gaps.

output_examples.input: Design login state management for a React dashboard.
output_examples.output: A concise AuthContext plan with user state, loading behavior, login and logout actions, and an initial session check.

output_examples.input: Review whether protected routes are enough for production.
output_examples.output: A risk-focused review that separates client navigation checks from server authorization and calls out missing token validation.

output_examples.input: Add OAuth and cookie sessions to a Next.js app.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step implementation outline covering provider setup, server-side session access, cookie behavior, and post-login redirects.

best_practices: Prefer httpOnly secure cookies for session tokens when the backend supports them.
Validate sessions or signed tokens on the server before returning protected data.
Pair cookie-based authentication with CSRF protection and strict cookie settings.
anti_patterns: Do not treat client-side route guards as the only access control.
Do not store long-lived bearer tokens in localStorage for production apps.
Do not copy placeholder credential or token functions without real validation.

faq.question: Does this skill implement authentication for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples that you adapt to your application and backend.

faq.question: Which storage option does it recommend for tokens?
faq.answer: It presents trade-offs and recommends httpOnly cookies as the safest default for many web apps.

faq.question: Can I use it with Next.js?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes NextAuth.js examples, App Router route protection, and security header guidance.

faq.question: Does it cover OAuth?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Google Identity Services and NextAuth.js provider patterns.

faq.question: Is the backend code complete?
faq.answer: No. The FastAPI section is a reference example and leaves credential checks and token creation to your app.

faq.question: What should I review before production use?
faq.answer: Review server-side authorization, token validation, CSRF protection, cookie settings, rate limits, and provider configuration.
